notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:


Thought

Here’s what stands out in your May 2026 spending and where you’ve got some easy wins.

1. Big picture
Total May spending: $9,256.08
No fees or interest charges this month, which is great.
You don’t have recurring items set up in Monarch yet, but you clearly have recurring bills (mortgage, daycare, insurance, streaming, etc.).

Your biggest categories are mostly true needs (mortgage, childcare, groceries, auto, insurance). The opportunity is more about tightening and plugging leaks than fixing any obvious bad habits.

2. Likely “waste” / optimization areas

Areas with room to tighten:

Groceries: Costco $736.80 + King Soopers $286.74 = $1,023.54
High for one month, likely some bulk/impulse buys or food waste.

Childcare: Bright Horizons $1,650 + Care.com Backup $220 = $1,870
Backup care suggests you’re paying a premium to cover gaps.

Insurance: State Farm Home $154 + Auto $218 = $372
Bundled policies often drift above market over time.

Subscriptions: Disney+ $15.99 + Spotify $19.99 = $35.98
Classic “small but recurring” category.

Dining: Blue Pan $78.35 + Snooze $86.40 = $164.75
Not huge, but easy to trim without a big lifestyle hit.

Household/Target: Target $242.19
Usually a mix of true needs and “Target run” extras.

Fitness: Peloton $44
Only wasteful if you’re not really using it.

Internet: Xfinity $119
Could be above promo pricing or include extras you don’t need.

Mortgage, auto loan, utilities, medical, HSA, and education savings all look intentional and not wasteful.

3. 5–10 concrete, prioritized actions

Here are specific moves, roughly ordered by savings potential and effort.

1) Tighten grocery spending and reduce food waste
Current: $1,023.54 (Costco + King Soopers).
Many similar households aim for $600–$800. You could:
Plan 4–5 dinners per week and shop from a list.
Cap Costco trips to a set budget and skip non‑food extras.
Do one “use‑up-the-pantry/freezer” week each month.
Estimated savings: $150–$300/month.
2) Re‑evaluate childcare and backup care
Current: $1,870 (Bright Horizons + Care.com backup).
Options:
Check if your employer offers subsidized backup care or a dependent care FSA.
Coordinate schedules to reduce backup hours (trade with another family, shift work hours).
If backup care is rare, build a trusted sitter list instead of paying for a premium service.
Estimated savings: $100–$200/month.
3) Shop your home and auto insurance
Current: $372/month with State Farm.
If you haven’t shopped in 2–3 years, you can often:
Get quotes from 2–3 other carriers for equivalent coverage.
Raise deductibles a bit if your emergency fund can handle it.
Estimated savings: $30–$75/month without cutting coverage quality.
4) Audit Target / “Household” spending
Current: $242.19 at Target.
Try:
Pull the receipt and tag each item as need vs nice‑to‑have.
Set a monthly cap for non‑essential household stuff (decor, toys, impulse).
Use pickup/online orders to avoid in‑store impulse buys.
If even 25–40% is non‑essential, that’s $60–$100/month you could keep.
5) Trim dining out while keeping what you enjoy
Current: $164.75 (Blue Pan + Snooze).
You could:
Keep one “favorite” outing per month and recreate the rest at home.
Use gift cards, happy hour, or split entrees to lower the bill.
Estimated savings: $50–$100/month.
6) Review streaming and digital subscriptions
Current: $35.98 (Disney+ + Spotify Family).
Actions:
Decide if you truly use both regularly.
Pause Disney+ between shows.
Drop to a cheaper Spotify tier or share a family plan with relatives.
Estimated savings: $10–$20/month.
7) Check Peloton value vs usage
Current: $44/month.
If you’re using it 3–4x/week, it’s probably worth it. If not:
Pause or cancel and swap to free/cheaper workout options.
Estimated savings: up to $44/month if it’s underused.
8) Negotiate or re‑shop internet
Current: $119/month with Xfinity.
Actions:
Call and ask for current promo pricing or a loyalty discount.
Drop unused add‑ons (extra speed tiers, equipment, TV bundles).
Estimated savings: $10–$30/month.
4. Potential total savings

If you make moderate changes (not extreme cuts), you’re looking at roughly:

Groceries: $200
Childcare/backup: $150
Insurance: $50
Household/Target: $80
Dining: $75
Subscriptions: $15
Peloton (if underused): $44
Internet: $20

That’s about ~$634/month in potential savings.

If you want, we can next sort these into “easy wins this month” vs “bigger structural changes”, or line them up with your priorities so you’re only cutting what matters least to you.
